findings.title: Unauthenticated health endpoint reveals per-component infrastructure status (DB/Redis) to any caller
findings.description: The /health endpoint is decorated @Public() and returns per-component status: { status, db, redis } in every response. On 200, it confirms both DB and Redis are reachable. On 503, it reveals exactly which dependency (db vs redis) is down. This endpoint is not rate-limited beyond the global defaults (30/s per IP) and requires no authentication. An attacker can infer infrastructure topology (what backing services are present) and monitor for partial outages in real time, which aids targeted timing attacks during degraded states.
findings.root_cause: HealthStatus interface exposes named component fields ('db', 'redis') directly in the HTTP body returned to any caller, with no authentication requirement.
findings.impact: Persistent attacker reconnaissance: confirms the tech stack uses PostgreSQL and Redis, monitors per-component availability without authentication. During a DB outage, attacker learns the DB is down (not Redis) — informing timing of follow-up attack vectors that exploit degraded state.
findings.suggested_fix: Return only { status: 'ok' | 'degraded' } to unauthenticated callers. Log per-component detail server-side only, or gate the granular payload behind a @Roles([ADMIN]) authenticated endpoint. Alternatively, return a minimal { ok: boolean } and rely on Prometheus metrics for operator drill-down.
acceptance_criteria: GET /api/health returns at most { status: 'ok' | 'degraded' } to unauthenticated callers — no 'db' or 'redis' fields
Per-component detail is logged server-side and/or exposed on an authenticated /api/health/detail endpoint
E2E smoke test verifies 200 body does not contain 'db' or 'redis' keys
findings.notes: The comment in health.service.ts explicitly says 'Does NOT expose process.uptime, NODE_ENV, or any internal process info' — but db/redis component names are internal process info and ARE exposed.

findings.title: Stdout audit log uses unkeyed plain SHA256 (8 chars) for attempted-login identifier — rainbow-table reversible for common emails
findings.description: For the stdout security log entry, attempted login emails are hashed with plain SHA256 (no HMAC key, no salt). The 8-character hex truncation reduces brute-force space but SHA256 is publicly computable and preimage-computable for any email in a dictionary. Server logs are 'broadly shipped' (per comments) to log aggregators like CloudWatch/ELK. An attacker with read access to logs can rainbow-table-reverse the 8-char hash against a list of known usernames/emails for the organization. Note: the persisted DB value uses a proper HMAC-keyed hash (hashAttemptedSubject) — only the stdout path is affected.
findings.root_cause: hashAttemptedLogin uses createHash('sha256') without a secret key, making it dictionary-reversible unlike the keyed HMAC used for DB persistence.
findings.impact: An attacker with read access to server logs (log aggregator breach, misconfigured S3 bucket) can recover attempted login targets for accounts with predictable email patterns, enabling targeted spear-phishing or account enumeration.
findings.suggested_fix: Use the same HMAC key (AUDIT_HASH_KEY) for the stdout digest as for DB persistence: createHmac('sha256', process.env.AUDIT_HASH_KEY).update(value.trim().toLowerCase()).digest('hex').slice(0, 8). This makes the stdout hash correlation-usable without being dictionary-reversible.
acceptance_criteria: hashAttemptedLogin is replaced with a keyed HMAC using AUDIT_HASH_KEY
Unit test verifies the stdout digest is not equal to unkeyed sha256 of the same input
AUDIT_HASH_KEY validation at boot (assertAuditHashKey) already covers this — no new boot assertion needed
findings.verification_command: N/A — manual verification: inspect audit.service.ts hashAttemptedLogin implementation
findings.notes: The DB persistence path correctly uses createHmac (hashAttemptedSubject, line 255). The stdout path is a deliberate simplification but could be upgraded to the same HMAC without breaking anything.

findings.title: $executeRawUnsafe used with string-interpolated constant for DDL trigger name — unsafe API pattern in maintenance scripts
findings.description: Two maintenance scripts (normalize-action-codes.ts, recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts) use $executeRawUnsafe with string interpolation of the IMMUTABILITY_TRIGGER constant ('audit_logs_no_update_delete'). While IMMUTABILITY_TRIGGER is a compile-time string constant — never user-supplied — using $executeRawUnsafe establishes a pattern that would immediately become a SQL injection sink if the trigger name were ever refactored to accept a parameter. Prisma $executeRaw tagged template cannot be used for DDL identifier interpolation (PostgreSQL does not support parameterized identifiers), so the safer long-term fix is format_ident() via a separate helper or a hardcoded non-parameterized string.
findings.root_cause: Prisma's $executeRaw tagged template cannot parameterize SQL identifiers (table/trigger names); the developer correctly chose $executeRawUnsafe but the string-interpolation pattern is risky if the constant is ever made dynamic.
findings.impact: No current impact since IMMUTABILITY_TRIGGER is a hardcoded string constant. Risk materializes only if a future refactor passes a caller-controlled trigger name to the function — which would become a full DDL injection.
findings.suggested_fix: Use a hardcoded string literal directly instead of interpolating the constant: `ALTER TABLE audit_logs DISABLE TRIGGER audit_logs_no_update_delete` — makes it obvious there is no dynamic component. Alternatively, add an allowlist assertion: if (IMMUTABILITY_TRIGGER !== 'audit_logs_no_update_delete') throw new Error('unexpected trigger name').
acceptance_criteria: DDL string in $executeRawUnsafe does not use string interpolation of any variable
Static analysis (eslint no-restricted-syntax or custom rule) flags $executeRawUnsafe with template literals
findings.notes: The same pattern appears in recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts lines 121-130. Both scripts require DATABASE_MIGRATION_URL (operator-only), so attacker reachability is very low.

verified_clean: apps/api/src/health/health.controller.ts — @Public() correct for monitoring; no sensitive fields in route handler; @ServiceUnavailableException body is the only concern (captured in S10-1)
apps/api/src/health/health.module.ts — no secrets, no SQL, no auth bypass

apps/api/src/deployments/deployments.service.ts — no controller/route exposure; parameterized SQL ($queryRaw tagged template); no hardcoded secrets; RELEASE_SHA/DEPLOYED_BY from env only; no user-supplied input paths
apps/api/src/deployments/deployments.module.ts — no issues

apps/api/src/testing/testing.controller.ts — @Public() with dual defense (module-level conditional import + handler-level NODE_ENV check); no DTOs or user input accepted; global ThrottlerBehindProxyGuard applies; design tradeoffs are intentional and documented (TST-017)
apps/api/src/testing/testing.module.ts — no issues

apps/api/src/audit/audit.service.ts — attemptedEmail excluded from loggable before stdout; BCRYPT_SHAPE guard prevents credential persistence; HMAC-keyed entityId for DB persistence; fire-and-forget pattern with .catch() ensures no crash on audit failure

apps/api/src/audit/audit-persistence.service.ts — advisory lock serializes chain inserts; all SQL uses parameterized $queryRaw/$executeRaw tagged templates; no user-controlled string reaches raw SQL; actorEmail/Label snapshot is a controlled DB lookup
apps/api/src/audit/audit-action.enum.ts — no security issues; compile-time closed enum
apps/api/src/audit/audit.module.ts — @Global() correct; no issues
apps/api/src/audit/export-audit.helper.ts — no user input; fire-and-forget with .catch(); payload fields are typed
apps/api/src/audit/payload-schemas.ts — Zod .strict() on every schema prevents unknown keys; AuditPayloadValidationError surfaces failures correctly; no injection vectors

apps/api/src/audit/recompute-chain.ts — all SQL uses parameterized $queryRaw/$executeRaw tagged templates; $executeRaw UPDATE uses parameterized values (${prevHash}, ${newRowHash}, ${row.id}); not user-reachable (operator script only)
apps/api/src/audit/audit-action.compile-witness.ts — compile-time only; no runtime security concerns
apps/api/src/audit/audit-payload-registry.compile-witness.ts — compile-time only; no runtime security concerns
apps/api/src/scripts/backfill-snapshots.ts — operator script; no user input; no SQL injection vectors
apps/api/src/scripts/system-backfill-audit.ts — typed payload; no user input; DEPLOYED_BY/DEPLOY_USER from env only

apps/api/src/scripts/normalize-action-codes.ts — data-bound SQL (FROM_VALUE, TO_VALUE) uses safe $executeRaw tagged template; $executeRawUnsafe only for DDL identifier (noted in S10-3); requires DATABASE_MIGRATION_URL (operator-only); dry-run rollback pattern is correct
apps/api/src/scripts/normalize-action-codes.witness.ts — test harness only; $executeRawUnsafe uses parameterized $1 placeholder for targetId; hardcoded EXPLAIN query in witness is static string
apps/api/src/scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts — same pattern as normalize-action-codes.ts; $executeRawUnsafe only for DDL (noted in S10-3); operator-only script
apps/api/src/scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.witness.ts — $queryRawUnsafe EXPLAIN call uses fully hardcoded SQL string with no interpolation
apps/api/src/schema-constraints/ — all files are *.int.spec.ts test files; no production code; no security concerns in scope
